01-01.text: Civil War: Confederate forces at Fort McRee and Fort Barrancas exchange heavy cannon fire with Union-held Fort Pickens.

01-02.text: Jimmy Simmon, Kathy Simmons and Kaye Wiggins are arrested in connection to the Christmas 1984 abortion clinic bombings.

01-03.text: Joseph Spence Roberts begins his first term as Escambia County Tax Collector.

01-04.text: The Pensacola Opera House opens at the corner of Government and Jefferson Streets.

01-05.text: Civil War: Florida's secession convention convenes in Tallahassee.

01-06.text: Civil War: State troops seize the federal arsenal at Apalachicola.

01-07.text: Civil War: State troops seized Fort Marion at St. Augustine. Adam Slemmer meets with the command of the Navy Yard as to what action should be taken in case local citizens and/or militia try to take possession of the Yard, or any of the forts.

01-08.text: A group of men under Colonel William H. Chase attempt to take control of the Union-held Fort Barrancas but are repelled by gunfire at around 11:00 PM.

01-09.text: Civil War: Federal troops receive orders to abandon the mainland's Fort Barrancas and refortify on Santa Rosa Island's Fort Pickens. The communication is not intercepted by Confederate troops because it was "in a pink, scented envelope addressed by a female hand."

01-10.text: Soccer complex corruption scandal: Following an add-on motion by Willie Junior, the Escambia County Commission votes 3-2 to purchase the defunct Stalnaker Mazda dealership for $2.3 million.

01-11.text: Civil War: Alabama secedes from the United States.

01-12.text: Civil War: Union forces under Commodore James Armstrong surrender the Navy Yard, including Fort Barrancas and Fort McRee, to Major William H. Chase and his Confederate troops. Navy Yard quartermaster William Conway refuses to strike the Union colors. William Franklin Lee joins the Confederacy.

01-13.text: An investigation by the State Attorney's Office concludes that a Pensacola Police Department raid of the Arety's Angels strip club received "far greater manpower and financing" than similar raids due to the influence of Escambia County Administrator George Touart.

01-14.text: Alcaniz Street north of Cervantes Street is renamed Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Drive.

01-15.text: Civil War: Fort Pickens refuses to surrender to William H. Chase and remains the only Union-controlled fort in the South for the duration of the war.

01-16.text: Matt Goldsby, Jimmy Simmons, Kaye Wiggins and Kathy Simmons are indicted on charges related to the Christmas 1984 abortion clinic bombings.

01-17.text: The U.S. Army appointed a Board of Engineers to consider a permanent deep channel at Pensacola Pass.

01-18.text: Civil War: For the third time, Florida Confederate officials demand the surrender of Union-held Fort Pickens.

01-19.text: Community Maritime Park: Raad Cawthon of E. W. Bullock Associates writes email describing five council members "in our pocket." This email was obtained through public records by park opponents and cited as evidence of impropriety.

01-20.text: The Pensacola City Council holds a reconsideration vote on their previous approval of the Community Maritime Park concept. It passes 8-2, with Jewel Cannada-Wynn joining Marty Donovan in dissent.

01-21.text: Stephen Russell Mallory retires from the U.S. Senate.

01-22.text: At the urging of Don Gilberto Antonio de St. Maxent, and hoping to undercut the British and American smuggling trade, Spain relaxes trade restrictions at Pensacola and other colonies, granting a 10-year license to trade with French ports.

01-24.text: Cemetery vandalism: Greg Allen Woodard causes thousands of dollars of damage at historic St. Michael's Cemetery, vandalizing at least two dozen graves in search of valuables to sell for cocaine. He was sentenced to 10 years in state prison.

01-25.text: French explorer Pierre LeMoyne d'Iberville arrives in Pensacola Bay and finds an existing Spanish colony.

01-26.text: Following a reprimand for financial misconduct, Congressman Bob Sikes is removed as chairman of the House Appropriations Subcommittee on Military Construction.

01-27.text: A mile-wide strip of Santa Rosa Island, centered on the Quarantine Station, is designated for use by the Marine Hospital Service.

01-28.text: Bernardo de Gálvez sails from New Orleans in an abortive attempt to capture Pensacola.

01-29.text: Civil War: With the United States Post Office at Pensacola abolished, the Alabama General Assembly votes to provide messengers for the Confederate troops stationed there.

01-30.text: First Spanish period: French ships under Pierre Le Moyne d'Iberville leave Spanish-occupied Pensacola Bay, going westward to found French Louisiana.

01-31.text: Daniel "Chappie" James, Jr., in Pensacola to receive a "Man of the Year" award, is refused a drink by Torch Lounge owner Jack Horne because of his race.Earnest N. Bracey. Daniel "Chappie" James. McFarland, 2003.

02-01.text: The First National Bank of Pensacola collapses when a Liverpool, England-based timber firm goes bankrupt.

02-02.text: An expedition under the command of Juan Enriques Barroto (Juan Jordan Reina) enters Pensacola Bay.

02-03.text: City officials enact an ordinance to pave Palafox Street in anticipation of the Pensacola Terminal Company's streetcar system.

02-04.text: Escambia High School riots: An impromptu election is held at Escambia High School in which students voted whether to keep the temporary name "Raiders" for their athletic teams, or revert to the name "Rebels," which had been deemed "racially irritating" by U.S. District Court Judge Winston E. Arnow. The students who preferred "Rebels" failed to obtain the needed supermajority, which led to the next day's riots.

02-05.text: Escambia High School riots: Following the previous days vote over the "Rebels" school mascot, a riot erupts at Escambia High School during which four students are shot and twenty-six students are injured by rocks and debris.

02-06.text: Civil War: The USS Brooklyn arrives at Santa Rosa Island with Union reinforcements for the besieged Fort Pickens.

02-07.text: Soccer complex corruption scandal: State Attorney Curtis Golden announces an investigation into the Escambia County Commission over possible corruption related to questionable land purchases.

02-08.text: The Bayfront Auditorium is completed, and the Pensacola High School band becomes the first group to play there (at the dedication ceremony).

02-09.text: William Panton writes to Alexander McGillivray regarding the theft of Panton, Leslie & Company goods by William Augustus Bowles and demanding "Satisfaction from the [Creek] Nation" and "the Life of that Villain Bowls."

02-10.text: Second Spanish period: The Spanish and British sign the Treaty of Paris, resulting in Spain ceding Florida to the British.

02-11.text: The Alabama & Florida Railroad Company is chartered.

02-12.text: Congress authorizes the President to "take possession of a tract of country … west of the River Perdido," resulting in the capture of Mobile.

02-14.text: The Alabama, Florida & Georgia Railroad Company is incorporated with the intent to build a railroad from Pensacola to the Florida-Alabama border, and thence to Columbus, Georgia.

02-15.text: The Pensacola Baptist Hospital Association is chartered by Dr. Sid Kennedy and a group of First Baptist Church deacons.

02-16.text: Tower East (originally the Pensacola Hospital and later renamed Sacred Heart Hospital) is added to the National Register of Historic Places.

02-17.text: Civil War: Florida becomes one of the Confederate States.

02-18.text: The Phineas Phogg's discotheque at Seville Quarter reopens after being damaged by a fire.

02-19.text: Transfer of Florida: The Adams-Onís Treaty is ratified for a second time by the U.S. Senate.

02-20.text: A complaint by Spanish citizens Marcos de Villiers and Arnaldo Guillemard, imprisoned by Territorial Governor George Walton, is read on the floor of the U.S. Senate.

02-21.text: Bentina Terry is appointed by Governor Charlie Crist to the Florida Technology, Research, and Scholarship Board.

02-22.text: Spain and the United States sign the Adams-Onis Treaty, giving the United States possession of Florida.

02-23.text: 15-year-old Daryl Williams is killed in a shooting incident at the Burger King on Pensacola Boulevard. Jamal Lee, 17, is later arrested and charged with murder.

02-24.text: At a demonstration protesting the Escambia County Sheriff's Department's refusal to discipline Deputy Doug Raines for killing a black motorist, a race riot erupts, resulting in the arrest of civil rights leaders B. J. Brooks and H. K. Matthews.

02-25.text: The Institute for Human and Machine Cognition, originally a research department for the University of West Florida, is restructured as a public research institute and incorporated as a Florida non-profit.

02-26.text: Michael Griffin murder trial: Houston anti-abortion activist Daniel Ware arrives in Pensacola and notifies John Burt of his plans to mount a suicide assault against a group of abortion providers who were in town for a memorial to David Gunn.

02-27.text: The Pensacola Railroad Company, incorporated exactly three years earlier and made up of Pensacola & Louisville Railroad assets purchased by Daniel F. Sullivan, is sold to the Louisville & Nashville Railroad.

02-28.text: Siege of Pensacola: Bernardo de Gálvez sails from New Orleans with an invasion force headed for Pensacola.

03-01.text: Following a scandal involving one of his aids, Admiral John H. Fetterman accepts a one-star demotion in rank (later restored) and retires from active duty

03-02.text: The Pensacola Historical Society holds its second meeting in the Shangtung Room of the San Carlos Hotel, where members ratify the organization's charter and bylaws.

03-03.text: Jackson Morton resigns his seat in the United States Senate.

03-04.text: Jackson Morton takes his seat as a Whig in the United States Senate.

03-05.text: The Pensacola Daily News prints its first issue for 2,500 subscribers.

03-06.text: The provisional municipality of Pensacola enters into a contract with the Pensacola Terminal Company for the paving of the streetcar system on Palafox Street.

03-07.text: Bloody Sunday: Pensacola civil rights activist H. K. Matthews is among those beaten and gassed by law enforcement officials in Selma.

03-08.text: The Floridian, having been purchased by John Fitzgerald and Co., recommences publication.

03-09.text: Bernardo de Galvez enters Pensacola Bay and lays siege to the British.

03-10.text: The Hyer-Knowles Planing Mill, having been stripped of machinery by Confederate General Braxton Bragg, is razed as part of the "scorched earth" evacuation. The surviving brick chimney is now part of Chimney Park.

03-11.text: Civil War: General Braxton Bragg arrives in Pensacola and assumes command of all Confederate forces.

03-12.text: Andrew Jackson is commissioned by President James Monroe to oversee the transfer of Florida to the United States and became Florida's first American governor.

03-13.text: Civil War: Braxton Bragg's Army of Pensacola is disbanded.

03-14.text: Battle of Fort Charlotte: Elias Durnford surrenders British Mobile to Spanish forces led by Bernardo de Gálvez.

03-15.text: George E. Wentworth is appointed Escambia County Tax Collector.

03-17.text: Elias Durnford joins the Royal Engineers and is made a Practitioner Engineer with rank of Ensign.

03-18.text: Siege of Pensacola: Four Spanish ships, including Bernardo de Gálvez's private brig Gálveztown, force entry into Pensacola Bay under fire from the British fort Red Cliffs.

03-19.text: Siege of Pensacola: Much of the Spanish invasion fleet forces entry into Pensacola Bay.

03-20.text: The Century Branch Library opens. Front Porch Pensacola community liaison Thelma Manley holds a press conference regarding accusations of misconduct. The Jubilee development is announced at a grandiose Saenger Theatre event.

03-21.text: Streetcar system: The Pensacola Electric Terminal Railway Company begins operation of electric-powered trolleys.

03-22.text: Siege of Pensacola: Spanish reinforcements under José de Expeleta arrive from Mobile at 9:30 a.m.

03-23.text: Siege of Pensacola – A contingent of fourteen Spanish vessels, carrying 1,348 troops, arrives from New Orleans. About 40 British sailors from the capsized Mentor are sent to bolster defenses at the Queen's Redoubt.

03-24.text: Siege of Pensacola: Spanish army forces ferry from Santa Rosa Island to the mainland behind the Red Cliffs fort.

03-25.text: Pez-Sigüenza expedition: Two ships depart Veracruz for Pensacola Bay.

03-26.text: Siege of Pensacola: Spanish troops advance from Tartar Point, enduring attacks by Indians allied to the British, to an encampment southwest of Sutton's Lagoon.

03-27.text: Creek War: Andrew Jackson's forces defeat the Red Sticks at the Battle of Horseshoe Bend.

03-28.text: At the recommendation of Andrés de Arriola, the Conde de Galve orders one hundred additional men to be conscripted from gambling houses and jails and sent to the Presidio Santa María de Galve.

03-29.text: The Army Corps of Engineers completes construction of improvements at the Navy Yard, including a battery for four 10-inch guns on disappearing carriages.

03-30.text: Siege of Pensacola: Bernardo de Gálvez leads a column of 1,100 men closer to the British Fort George and its redoubts.

03-31.text: A. M. Avery's Hardware Company merges with the Gordon & Brown Hardware Company and is reincorporated as the Avery Hardware & Supply Company.

04-01.text: Christ Church Parish holds a final service in Old Christ Church, on Good Friday, before moving to the new church building.

04-02.text: Democratic primary elections: Frank Reilly is elected Mayor of Pensacola and Frank Dent Sanders is elected Pensacola City Marshal.

04-03.text: The first service is held at the newly built Christ Episcopal Church on Wright Street.

04-04.text: Escambia County Sheriff Vince Seely is found to have a blood-alcohol level of .23 percent after running a stop sign and causing a three-vehicle collision. He had a previous wreck on January 29, but claimed an oncoming car ran him off the road and was not tested.

04-05.text: Ever'man Natural Foods Co-op, Inc. is incorporated.

04-06.text: Presidio Santa María de Galve: René-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle, claims Louisiana for France.

04-07.text: A Spanish expedition led by Andrés de Pez enters Pensacola Bay.

04-08.text: The Pez-Sigüenza expedition begins an examination of Pensacola Bay, investigating landforms they named "Punta de Sigüenza" and "Barranca de Santo Tomé."

04-09.text: Luna expedition: Ángel de Villafañe becomes governor of Florida.

04-10.text: Streetcar operators' strike – Strikebreakers hired by the Pensacola Electric Company begin arriving in the city.

04-11.text: Streetcar operators' strike: The Florida state militia arrives in Pensacola to protect strikebreakers and restore order.

04-12.text: Spain signs a treaty with France, obligating the latter to declare war against England and help, among other goals, return Mobile and Pensacola to Spanish rule.

04-13.text: War of 1812: Spanish Captain Cayetano Pérez surrenders Fort Carlota (Mobile) to American General James Wilkinson.

04-14.text: Streetcar operators' strike: The Pensacola Electric Company resumes limited streetcar service on two lines, with cars operated by strikebreakers and protected by state militia troops.

04-15.text: Local group the Sandpipers perform with the Allman Joys at the Firemans Hall.

04-16.text: Civil War: The USS Atlantic arrives off Santa Rosa Island and disembarks 1,000 men for the defense of Fort Pickens.

04-17.text: Fort Pickens receives supplies and troop reinforcements from the USS Powhatan. Confederate Brigadier General Braxton E. Bragg imposes martial law in Pensacola, stopping all trade and communications with the Union-held fortress.

04-18.text: Henry Wilson is appointed Sheriff of Escambia County

04-19.text: Presidio Santa María de Galve: A Spanish royal cédula affirms the priority of establishing a colony at Panzacola.

04-20.text: French period: The Sieur de Bienville, French governor at Mobile, receives orders to seize Pensacola.

04-21.text: Streetcar operators' strike: A crowd fires upon a streetcar on the West Hill line and fatally wounds the conductor, a Mr. G. Hoffman.

04-22.text: The site of the Navy Yard is reserved by an act of Congress.

04-23.text: Andrew Jackson writes to Pensacola's Spanish governor Mauricio de Zuñiga regarding the Negro Fort on the Apalachicola River.

04-24.text: Streetcar operators' strike: Twenty-four Pensacola police officers refuse to cross picket lines to protect strikebreakers. Two-thirds of the police force are fired over the incident.

04-25.text: Streetcar operators' strike: With public opinion turned against the strikers, the Pensacola Electric Company is able to operate all streetcar lines again.

04-26.text: Major General Andrew Jackson convenes a court martial at Fort St. Marks for two British subjects, Alexander Arbuthnot and Robert C. Ambrister, charged with inciting Seminole Indians against the United States.

04-27.text: History of healthcare: Surgeon Lorimer writes to Frederick Haldimand with an estimate for establishing a hospital in British Pensacola.

04-28.text: Siege of Pensacola: Spanish engineers begin digging a trench near the British Queen's Redoubt.

04-29.text: Siege of Pensacola: Spanish forces open fire on the Queen's Redoubt with four battery mortars.

04-30.text: Fernando Moreno requests to be retired from the Spanish Army.

05-01.text: Siege of Pensacola: The Spanish install six 24-pounders in the advance battery.

05-02.text: Pensacola Baptist Church of Christ (the predecessor of First Baptist Church) is organized.

05-03.text: Freemasonry: St. Andrew's Lodge No. 1 is founded by authority of a charter in British Pensacola.

05-04.text: U.S. District Court Judge Roger Vinson takes a seat on the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Court.

05-05.text: Monkees band members Micky Dolenz and Davy Jones perform, with Micky's sister Coco, at Big Daddy's Lounge near Cordova Mall.

05-06.text: The Pez-Sigüenza expedition begins its return trip to Veracruz.

05-07.text: Civil War: Upon learning that Union commander David Farragut's fleet was anchored off Mobile Bay, Colonel Thomas Jones quickly removes most of the Confederate artillery and supplies from Pensacola.

05-08.text: Siege of Pensacola: The powder magazine at the Queen's Redoubt is ignited by Spanish troops, forcing British General John Campbell to surrender Fort George and Pensacola to Bernardo de Gálvez.

05-09.text: Siege of Pensacola: The terms of British Pensacola's surrender are negotiated.

05-10.text: Siege of Pensacola: Capitulation of British forces. Pensacola's third Spanish period begins.

05-11.text: W. A. Blount submits a Florida Senate version of State Representative John Campbell Avery, Jr.'s "Jim Crow" bill to segregate Florida streetcars.

05-12.text: A "Jim Crow" law to segregate streetcars, authored by Pensacola legislators John Campbell Avery, Jr. and W. A. Blount, passes both Florida houses unanimously.

05-13.text: French forces led by brothers Jean-Baptiste Le Moyne, Sieur de Bienville and Pierre Le Moyne d'Iberville depart Mobile for Pensacola.

05-14.text: French Pensacola: With little resistance from the Spanish garrison, Jean-Baptiste Le Moyne de Bienville captures Pensacola for France.

05-15.text: Civil War: General Braxton Bragg increases the garrison at Fort Barrancas from 7,000 to 10,000 men.

05-16.text: Andrés de Arriola arrives in Pensacola Bay with food supplies to reinforce the Presidio Santa María de Galve, which had previously borrowed provisions from French Mobile.

05-17.text: The American Continental Congress orders all trade with British West Florida stopped.

05-18.text: A Spanish royal order grants Bernardo de Gálvez authority to invade British West Florida.

05-19.text: The place name "Panzacola" is mentioned in a letter by Lt. Antonio Matheos.

05-20.text: A fire destroys much of Milton's business district.

05-21.text: Western end of Santa Rosa Island (Punta de Sigüenza) transferred to U.S. War Department by Executive Order.

05-22.text: The Barroto-Romero expedition reaches Mobile Bay and begins a two-day exploration.

05-23.text: Battle of Pensacola: Andrew Jackson exchanges correspondence with Spanish Governor Jose Masot, demanding the surrender of Fort San Carlos de Barrancas.

05-24.text: Battle of Pensacola: Andrew Jackson captures Pensacola.

05-25.text: Frank Patti, indicted on tax fraud charges, crashes his pickup truck into the Frisco 1355 engine on Garden Street.

05-26.text: The First Methodist Church purchases a lot at Tarragona and Intendencia Streets for the congregation's first permanent building.

05-27.text: The state-appointed Provisional Municipality of Pensacola government ends with a new city charter.

05-28.text: Battle of Pensacola: Governor Jose Masot capitulates Spanish Pensacola to Andrew Jackson.

05-29.text: Andrew Jackson issues a proclamation of his reasons for occupying Pensacola.

05-30.text: Matt Goldsby and Jimmy Simmons are sentenced to ten years in prison for the Christmas abortion bombings, while Kaye Wiggins and Kathy Simmons received five years' probation.

05-31.text: Nicole Pfister is booked for allegedly forging Gulf Power documents related to former Front Porch Pensacola community liaison Thelma Manley.

06-01.text: Third Spanish period: Following the successful siege, much of the Spanish fleet departs Pensacola.

06-02.text: Andrew Jackson writes to John C. Calhoun about his capture of Pensacola and announces the Seminole War was over.

06-03.text: A stone marker is erected in Seville Square by the Seville Square Childhood Reunion Group and dedicated to "all those who spent their youth in and around Seville Square."

06-04.text: Bernardo de Gálvez names Arturo O'Neill governor of Spanish West Florida.

06-05.text: British West Florida authorities pass an act to establish a ferry at the Perdido River and build a road from Pensacola to Mobile Bay.

06-06.text: "White Democratic Party" candidates (including Charles H. Bliss for mayor) make a clean sweep in municipal elections.

06-07.text: The State of Florida incorporates the Pensacola & Birmingham Railroad Company.

06-08.text: A land expedition led by Laureano de Torres y Ayala departs Apalache for Pensacola.

06-09.text: Andrew Jackson and José Callava open formal discussions regarding the transfer of Florida to the United States.

06-10.text: Congress authorizes the replacement of Pensacola's federal buildings destroyed in the fire of 1880.

06-11.text: Luna expedition: Tristán de Luna sets out from Veracruz, Mexico with thirteen ships carrying 500 soldiers, 1,000 colonists and servants, and 240 horses.

06-12.text: George C. Scudamore, arrested for embezzling funds from the Pensacola Bank & Trust Company, is declared sane by a Florida asylum and jailed in Pensacola to await trial.

06-13.text: Presidio Santa María de Galve: A Spanish royal cédula is issued ordering the immediate occupation of Pensacola Bay.

06-14.text: The City of Pensacola passes an ordinance "to regulate the production and sale, and to perfect the purity of milk."

06-15.text: Andrew Jackson moves into Florida and establishes himself at Gonzalia, home of Manuel Gonzalez, to oversee the transfer of Florida.

06-16.text: Revolutionary War: Hoping to regain its Florida colonies, Spain joins the fledgling United States of America by declaring war against England.

06-17.text: The Confederate monument at Lee Square is dedicated in a grand ceremony attended by thousands of visitors from around the state.

06-18.text: War of 1812: The United States declares war against England.

06-19.text: ECSO deputies Nick Harris, Matt Clark, Steve Nesmith and retired detective Dan Henry are cleared by a jury in a civil lawsuit alleging they illegally searched Jamareo Durr's residence.

06-20.text: A fire in Fort Pickens detonates 8,000 pounds of powder in the Bastion D magazine, killing one soldier, reducing the entire bastion to rubble and propelling bricks from its walls as far as Fort Barrancas across the bay.

06-21.text: Spain declares war on England, threatening British West Florida.

06-22.text: Services at the first home of First United Methodist Church (at Tarragona and Intendencia Streets) are held for the first time.

06-23.text: Following a string of racial violence, the Escambia County School Board rules it could decide the nickname of Escambia High's sports mascot. Hours later, seven crosses are burned in front of black churches, the SCLC offices, the WEAR studios and the home of WEAR reporter Ken Larson.

06-24.text: The Pensacola & Louisville Railroad Company commences construction of a telegraph line along its road.

06-25.text: A confidential letter from King George III and Lord Germain to John Campbell, instructing him to seize New Orleans, is intercepted by Bernardo de Gálvez.

06-26.text: First Spanish period: A Spanish royal cédula instructs the Conde de Galve to begin a detailed examination of Pensacola Bay.

06-27.text: Captain William P. Reavey, Jr. is installed as commanding officer of Naval Air Station Pensacola, replacing Captain Peter S. Frano.

06-28.text: Transfer of Florida to the United States: Andrew Jackson sends his wife Rachel from Gonzalia to occupy the Spanish Governor's house vacated by Jose Callava.

06-29.text: Jo Hovind, wife of Kent Hovind, is sentenced to one year and one day in federal prison, stemming from her conviction on 45 counts of evading bank-reporting requirements.

06-30.text: Congress approves $40,000 in funds to complete construction of Fort Pickens and $50,000 toward construction of Fort McRee.

07-01.text: Florida's first Jim Crow law, written by Pensacolian John Campbell Avery, Jr. and segregating streetcars, goes into effect.

07-02.text: Spanish expedition under Laureano del Torres y Ayala arrived at Pensacola Bay via an overland route from Apalachicola.

07-03.text: Anti-abortion activist John Burt settles a civil lawsuit with the family of murdered abortion provider David Gunn in which he lost possession of land adjacent to the Ladies Center used for protesting.

07-04.text: Father de Veley of St. Michael's Parish conducts his first funeral, for Anthony Soler.

07-05.text: A hurricane hits Pensacola, causing more than $1 million in estimated damages, including extensive damage to the Opera House.

07-06.text: County Attorney Stephen Russell Mallory is authorized to negotiate the purchase of a lot at Palafox and Chase Streets to be used for a County Courthouse.

07-07.text: J. A. McMann and W. F. Mershon, two cadets in flight training, are killed when their planes collide near NAS Pensacola.

07-08.text: Jonathan Walker's ship is rescued by two wrecking sloops, and he and the seven escaped slaves he was transporting were taken to Key West.

07-09.text: The Provisional Municipality of Pensacola passes a resolution allowing the Southern Bell Telephone & Telegraph Company to erect poles in the city.

07-10.text: Second Spanish period: Colonel Augustine Prevost sails from Havana, Cuba with four ships and 350 men on their way to accept the transfer of Florida from Spain to Great Britain.

07-11.text: Senator John C. Calhoun advocates for a $95,000 appropriation to establish a dry dock at Pensacola.

07-12.text: Andrew Jackson begins correspondence with Spanish Governor Mateo Gonzáles Manrique, demanding extradition of Creek warriors who had taken refuge in Florida.

07-13.text: Governor Andrés de Arriola brings supplies to the Presidio Santa María de Galve from Veracruz.

07-14.text: A Spanish royal cédula reaches Mexico with orders to occupy Pensacola Bay and establish a colony there.

07-15.text: President James Monroe meets with his Cabinet to decide what to do about Andrew Jackson's recent capture of Spanish Pensacola.

07-16.text: The Pensacola & Louisville Railroad Company is incorporated by the Florida legislaure.

07-17.text: U.S. General Andrew Jackson accepts the transfer of Florida from Spain to the United States in Plaza Ferdinand VII and installs a provisional capital in Pensacola.

07-18.text: General Andrew Jackson appoints a city constable, effectively forming the Pensacola Police Department.

07-19.text: Brandon Ward is sentenced to life in prison for the murder of Joseph Hall. The Escambia County Commission unanimously passes an ordinance modifying the limits on condominiums at Pensacola Beach and Perdido Key.

07-20.text: The Torres expedition completes its examination of Pensacola Bay.

07-21.text: Civil War: 1,100 Union soldiers under Brigadier General Alexander Asboth depart Fort Barrancas to rendezvous with Sherman's Army.

07-22.text: Antebellum period: The first official session of Florida's Territorial Legislature Council is convened in Pensacola.

07-23.text: Spanish Pensacola and its Forts Barrancas and San Miguel are occupied by a British force led by Lieutenant-Colonel Edward Nichols.

07-24.text: Congress passes legislation giving authority to any organized company to construct telegraph lines, later conflicting with exclusive charters granted by Florida to the Pensacola Telegraph Company.

07-25.text: Brigadier General Alexander Asboth and his troops return to Fort Barrancas following the Battle of Fort Hodgson.

07-26.text: Confederate President Jefferson Davis appoints Thomas E. Jordan as postmaster of Pensacola and Chandler Cox Yonge as attorney for the Florida district of the Confederacy.

07-27.text: Battle of Burnt Corn marks the beginning of the Creek War.

07-28.text: The Pensacola Terminal Company is granted a right of way through the Fort Barrancas Military Reservation.

07-29.text: The state Jim Crow law, authored by Pensacola legislators to segregate streetcars, is struck down as unconstitutional by the Florida Supreme Court.

07-30.text: A memorial plaque to preservationist R. N. Dunagan is dedicated in Chimney Park.

07-31.text: Elias Durnford is named acting Lieutenant Governor of British West Florida.

08-01.text: The segregated Belmont Theatre, previously catering exclusively to blacks, opens its doors to whites, who are required to sit in the balcony.

08-02.text: King Charles II of Spain orders the governor of Florida to cooperate with Martin de Echegaray in the exploration of Bahía de Espíritu Santu.

08-03.text: Spain transfers title to Florida to Britain in exchange for the return of the City of Havana, Cuba.

08-05.text: Lieutenant Colonel Augustine Prevost arrives in Pensacola with 350 men to accept the transfer of Florida from Spain to Great Britian.

08-06.text: British troops take control of Pensacola and West Florida.

08-07.text: French Pensacola surrenders unconditionally to Spanish forces.

08-08.text: Rock bands Foreigner, Loverboy, Ozzy Osbourne, and LeRoux perform at City Park Stadium in New Orleans, Louisiana.

08-09.text: U.S. General Andrew Jackson signs the Treaty of Fort Jackson with the Creek nation.

08-10.text: Peter Chester arrived in Pensacola as governor of British West Florida.

08-11.text: The Poarch Band of Creek Indians are recognized as an Indian Tribe by the U.S. Department of Interior and the Bureau of Indian Affairs.

08-12.text: Naval Aeronautical Station aviators Garrett T. Mandeville, August Copp, and Guy B. Fraley are killed when their seaplane crashes into Pensacola Bay.

08-13.text: Yellow fever is announced in Pensacola, forcing the Florida legislative council to relocate elsewhere.

08-14.text: A Spanish expedition led by Tristán de Luna y Arellano enters Pensacola Bay.

08-15.text: The Spanish expedition led by Tristán de Luna y Arellano lands, establishing the first European settlement within the continental United States.

08-16.text: The Escambia County Commission votes to take over full control of the West Florida Public Library system, while shifting the Escambia County Area Transit bus system to the City of Pensacola, which had not yet approved the deal.

08-17.text: Spanish forces begin an assault upon French-held Dauphin Island and Mobile.

08-18.text: The first issue of The Floridian is published by George Tunstall and Cary Nicholas.

08-19.text: Pensacola police officer Archie Bowman, killed while pursuing a suspect

08-20.text: Civil War: Admiral David Farragut arrives in Pensacola.

08-21.text: Andrew Jackson orders a search of the home of Domingo Sousa for official papers not turned over by Spanish governor Jose Callava during the transfer of Florida.

08-22.text: Andrew Jackson has former Spanish Governor José Callava imprisoned over the latter's withholding of certain documents during the transfer of Florida.

08-23.text: Escambia County Commissioners cancel the planned purchase of a $1.4 million property following disclosures of a relationship between the sellers and County Administrator George Touart.

08-24.text: Brigadier General Henry Bouquet arrives in Pensacola to command British West Florida; he died of disease on September 3.

08-25.text: War of 1812: Spanish officials permit 300 British troops to land at Pensacola and anchor a fleet in the harbor.

08-27.text: War of 1812: Andrew Jackson arrives in Mobile and requests the 2,500-man Tennessee militia be sent to defend against British forces at Spanish Pensacola.

08-28.text: Colonel Edward A. Perry of the Confederate 2nd Florida Infantry Regiment is promoted to brigadier general.

08-29.text: War of 1812: British Major Edward Nicholls issues a proclamation from Spanish Pensacola urging Louisiana natives to assist him "in liberating [their land] from a faithless, imbecile government."

08-30.text: Construction begins on the fort stockade at Presidio San Miguel de Panzacola.

08-31.text: Patrick Nobles is sentenced to ten years in state prison for violating the terms of his probation related to the August 20, 2003 DUI-manslaughter death of Todd Currie.

09-01.text: Felipe de Orbalaes, a friar at Nuestra Señora de las Angustias, is killed in an Indian raid outside Fort San Carlos de Austria.

09-02.text: Civil War: A Federal raiding party ferries from Fort Pickens to burn a dry dock at the Confederate-held Navy Yard.

09-03.text: Spanish forces evacuate British-controlled Pensacola to Vera Cruz.

09-04.text: ECUA receives a major permit required to begin building the Central Water Reclamation Facility to replace the Main Street plant.

09-05.text: British West Florida Governor Peter Chester writes an official endorsement of visiting botanist William Bartram.

09-06.text: Escambia County Administrator George Touart steps down amidst criticism of his personal business dealings. The Florida Supreme Court rules for the plaintiff in Strand v. Escambia County, declaring the Southwest Escambia Improvement District unconstitutional.

09-08.text: General John Campbell at Pensacola learns that Spain had declared war on Britain several months earlier, on June 21.

09-09.text: U.S. District Court Judge Roger Vinson blocks enforcement of the Escambia County Commission's ban on the The Last Temptation of Christ, allowing the film to open at the Plaza III Theatres.

09-10.text: The Pensacola City Council's Committee of the Whole approves an additional $6 million in funding to renovate the Saenger Theatre.

09-11.text: The 9-11 Homage to America memorial statue on the Palafox Street median is dedicated.

09-13.text: President John Tyler appoints Ebenezer Dorr IV U.S. Marshal of the Western District of the Territory of Florida.

09-14.text: Battle of Santa Rosa Island: A Federal raiding party casts off from the USS Colorado and sets fire to the Confederate privateering schooner Judah.

09-15.text: Franklin "Kim" Kimbrough begins work as executive director of the Downtown Improvement Board. Thelma Manley takes $3,393 in Front Porch Pensacola funds to pay back taxes from 2001 on a piece of personal property.

09-16.text: The Pensacola Chamber of Commerce meets for the first time and William Dudley Chipley becomes its first president.

09-17.text: French Pensacola: For the second time that year, the Spanish garrison at Presidio Santa María de Galve surrenders to a French attack.

09-18.text: Henry Clay Armstrong is removed as mayor by an 8-1 city council vote. W. L. Moyer is appointed the new mayor, but dies later that evening.

09-19.text: A hurricane makes landfall, wrecking the supply-laden fleet of the Luna expedition in Pensacola Bay.

09-20.text: A hurricane makes landfall near Pensacola, causing extensive damage to the area.

09-21.text: British West Florida Governor George Johnstone (along with Elias Durnford and others) set sail from Jamaica to Pensacola.

09-22.text: The Amtrak Sunset Limited derails over the Big Bayou Canot near Mobile, killing five crew members and forty-two passengers.

09-24.text: Ensign J. P. Bournique and machinists Thomas O. Jones and E. B. Saunders are killed when a seaplane crashes at the Naval Aeronautical Station.

09-25.text: Community Redevelopment Agency: The Pensacola City Council adopts a resolution to revitalize the city's blighted core.

09-26.text: The Saenger Theatre reopens after restoration.

09-27.text: A hurricane makes landfall west of Mobile.

09-28.text: The Pensacola City Council votes 5-4 to cancel a planned referendum on increasing the Downtown Improvement Board district, citing an undue burden on the residents who might be affected by the additional taxes. Congress designates the downtown post office in honor of late Mayor Vince Whibbs.

09-29.text: Territorial governor Andrew Jackson issues a proclamation banishing several Spanish citizens, including Marcos de Villiers and Arnaldo Guillemard, from the city.

09-30.text: J. Earle Bowden begins his career at the Pensacola News Journal.

10-01.text: WEAR news anchor Bob Solarski is arrested on DUI charges after damaging several vehicles in the University Mall parking lot.

10-02.text: Soccer complex corruption scandal: Willie Junior pays off many of his business debts with a single payment of nearly $300,000.

10-03.text: A farewell party is given in honor of outgoing territorial governor Andrew Jackson and his wife Rachel at Austin's Tavern.

10-04.text: Jerry's Cajun Café reopens after being damaged by a fire. The Pensacola News Journal reveals that Escambia County School Board member Jeff Bergosh was the pnj.com user "Godzilla" responsible for several controversial comments related to the school district.

10-05.text: The Escambia County School District's administrative building on Garden Street is named in honor of educator Vernon McDaniel.

10-07.text: The Cervantes Street bridge spanning Bayou Texar is dedicated in honor of dentist and civic activist Philip Payne.

10-08.text: Battle of Santa Rosa Island: A Confederate force of over 1,000 men under general Richard H. Anderson set out from Pensacola, landing on Santa Rosa Island about four miles east of Union-held Fort Pickens.

10-09.text: Battle of Santa Rosa Island: A surprise raid by Confederate forces upon Fort Pickens is repelled by Union troops.

10-10.text: The Overby Enterprises building at Palafox and Main Streets suffers a fire. The building was later demolished.

10-11.text: Grand Opening of Westwood Mall, later to dba Mariner Mall, located at Fairfield Drive and Mobile Highway.

10-12.text: The thirteenth storm of the 1916 hurricane season forms in the western Caribbean.

10-13.text: The Imogene Theater in Milton opened it's doors for the first time.

10-14.text: Protestant Episcopal Congregation of the City of Pensacola petitioned the Territory of Florida to incorporate Christ Church Parish.

10-15.text: Presidio Santa María de Galve: Three ships under Andrés de Arriola depart Vera Cruz to secure Pensacola Bay for Spain and drive out any French presence they might encounter.

10-16.text: A Spanish invasion force led by Bernardo de Gálvez departs Havana for British Pensacola, but is scattered by a hurricane in the gulf.

10-17.text: A group of over a dozen male Pensacola High School students, many on the Tigers football team, coerce a female student into performing oral sex on many of them in a school bathroom and locker room, leading to an alleged coverup by school officials.

10-18.text: A hurricane hits Pensacola for the second time in one season.

10-19.text: Congressman Bob Sikes temporarily resigns from office to serve in the U.S. Army during World War II.

10-20.text: The line and other assets of the Pensacola Railroad Company are sold to the Louisville & Nashville Railroad.

10-21.text: British West Florida's first governor George Johnstone and surveyor general Elias Durnford arrive in Pensacola.

10-22.text: Confederate troops under Major General Braxton Bragg organize as the Army of Pensacola.

10-23.text: Rodney Kendig begins his tenure as Escambia County Administrator.

10-24.text: Revolutionary War: The Continental Congress writes to the Assembly in British Pensacola, but is suppressed by Governor Peter Chester.

10-25.text: The Board of Navy Commissioners arrives in Pensacola to survey sites for the Navy Yard.

10-26.text: Secretary of State John Quincy Adams responds to U.S. territorial judge Eligius Fromentin over his grievances with Andrew Jackson.

10-27.text: Lewis Warrington ends his tenure as commandant of the Navy Yard.

10-28.text: Civil War: The USS Montgomery seizes the blockade running steamer Caroline off the coast of Mobile; Union soldiers surprise a detachment of Confederate cavalry at Gonzalia, capturing or killing all but nine.

10-29.text: The Gulf Power Company is organized as a subsidiary of the Southeastern Power & Light holding company, serving areas east of Pensacola.

10-30.text: Tristán de Luna y Arellano is named by the Viceroy of Mexico, Luis De Velasco, to head the Spanish expedition to occupy Florida.

10-31.text: The Philip Dane Beall Sr. Memorial Bridge over Pensacola Bay is dedicated, replacing the 30-year-old Thomas A. Johnson Bridge (which was converted to a fishing pier).

11-01.text: The Halloween Night Fire destroys the structures on the west side of Palafox Street between Garden and Romana Streets.

11-02.text: Elections: In addition to the U.S. presidential race, several city and other local offices are decided, and a ballot measure by AccessEscambia is defeated.

11-03.text: A hurricane destroys the Spanish presidio on Santa Rosa Island.

11-04.text: A three-person board consisting of James Biddle, Lewis Warrington, and William Bainbridge recommend the construction of a Navy Yard at Pensacola.

11-05.text: Henry C. Mustin makes the first catapult launch of an aircraft from the USS North Carolina anchored off the Naval Aeronautical Station.

11-06.text: Capture of Pensacola: Andrew Jackson arrives at Pensacola with a contingent of American troops and attempts to communicate with the Spanish governor, Mateo Gonzáles Manrique, but is fired upon.

11-07.text: Capture of Pensacola: Andrew Jackson marches on Pensacola, and within minutes the city is surrendered by Spanish governor Mateo Gonzáles Manrique.

11-08.text: Capture of Pensacola: As Andrew Jackson secures the Spanish city, British forces retreat from Fort San Carlos de Barrancas, detonating the harbor defenses.

11-09.text: Capture of Pensacola: After driving the British from Spanish Pensacola, Andrew Jackson returns the city to the control of Mateo Gonzáles Manrique.

11-10.text: 485 acres of the Ferry Pass Heights area is annexed into the City of Pensacola. Department store magnate James Cash Penney arrived for the opening of the newly-remodeled JC Penney store located downtown.

11-11.text: The ACLU of Florida Panhandle Chapter is restarted in Pensacola, its previous charter having expired in 1995.

11-12.text: Andrew Jackson ends his tenure as territorial governor of Florida.

11-13.text: Andrew Jackson submits his resignation as territorial governor to President James Monroe.

11-14.text: Civil War: A Federal expedition from Pensacola, which included five boats, a special work gang, and sixty U.S. marines, heads along the coast to St. Andrews Bay with orders to destroy all salt works located between the two ports.

11-15.text: The Pensacola City Council passes an ordinance allowing steel track for a streetcar system to be placed in the roadways.

11-16.text: Jonathan Walker's sentence for slave stealing, including having his hand branded, is carried out by Ebenezer Dorr IV.

11-17.text: Presidio Santa María de Galve: Juan Jordán de Reina arrives in Pensacola Bay.

11-18.text: Tarkiln Field near Perdido Bay is commissioned as NAAS Bronson Field, in honor of Naval Aviator Clarence K. Bronson.

11-19.text: The first session of the Senate of the Spanish Republic of West Florida is held in Francisville, Louisiana.

11-20.text: George Johnstone is appointed governor of British West Florida.

11-21.text: Andrés de Arriola arrives in Pensacola Bay to establish the Presidio Santa María de Galve.

11-22.text: Civil War: Union-held Fort Pickens exchanges artillery fire with Confederate batteries at the Navy Yard and Fort McRee.

11-23.text: Civil War: Union-held Fort Pickens continues to exchange fire with Confederate Forts Barrancas and McRee.

11-24.text: British Pensacola: The first British council is held.

11-25.text: Second Spanish period: The Presidio Isla de Santa Rosa is established.

11-26.text: Two small areas, one northwest of Strong and M Streets and another southeast of la Rua and T Streets, are annexed by petition into the City of Pensacola.

11-28.text: Rock band Kansas, with special guests The Rockets, perform at the Mobile Municipal Auditorium.

11-29.text: Landmark Skating Rink, formerly known as Dreamland Skate Center, located at 1002 N Navy Blvd, is destroyed by fire.

11-30.text: Jackson Morton is chosen to represent Santa Rosa County as a delegate of the Florida Secession Convention in Tallahassee.

12-01.text: Confederate General Braxton E. Bragg reports that he has approximately 3,000 men under his command in Pensacola, but arms for only about 600 of them.

12-03.text: President John Quincy Adams confirms the selection of Pensacola as the site for the construction of a United States Navy Yard.

12-04.text: The site for the Pensacola Navy Yard is approved by President John Quincy Adams

12-05.text: The 'USS Pensacola''''' is commissioned at the Pensacola Navy Yard.

12-06.text: Daniel "Chappie" James, Jr. assumes command as special assistant to the chief of staff, U.S. Air Force.

12-07.text: A new city plan by Vicente Sebastián Pintado, Surveyor General of Spanish West Florida, is approved by the Pensacola City Council.

12-08.text: Escambia County School Board member Vanette Webb is indicted for allegedly withholding documents from parent Susan Watson, in violation of Florida's open record laws.

12-09.text: Joseph Forsyth transfers half his land holdings to Andrew and Ezekiel Simpson, and together the three men establish the Arcadia Manufacturing Company.

12-10.text: The Pensacola Electric Company activates the first electric lights in the city at 7:00PM.

12-11.text: A fire erupts in downtown Pensacola, destroying five blocks of the main business district and over 100 buildings and businesses.

12-12.text: A new bridge carrying Interstate 10 over Escambia Bay is dedicated by Governor Charlie Crist in honor of engineer David Bogan.

12-13.text: The Pensacola Sports Association is incorporated.

12-14.text: Andrews Institute founder James Andrews agrees to pay $450,000 of a $14.9 million settlement with the federal government on charges of fraud and illegal kickbacks.

12-15.text: Does v. School Board for Santa Rosa County, Florida: The Santa Rosa County School Board, along with Superintendent Tim Wyrosdick and Pace High School principal Frank Lay, sign an "Admission of Liability" regarding district-wide constitutional violations. The merger of Synovus companies Bank of Pensacola and Vanguard Bank and Trust into Coastal Band and Trust is completed.

12-16.text: After being boarded up for nearly two years, Seville Quarter reopens under the ownership of Wilmer Mitchell and family.

12-17.text: Manuel Gonzalez joins the Spanish army, which sends him to the New World.

12-19.text: President Martin Van Buren dismisses Thomas Eastin from his post as navy agent at the Port of Pensacola for having "failed to settle [his] accounts as required by law."

12-20.text: The final issue of The Floridian is published by John Fitzgerald & Company.

12-21.text: Maghen Davala and Robert Ellingson are arrested for allegedly trying to sell their 2-month-old baby in a PetSmart parking lot for $30.

12-23.text: Spanish King Ferdinand VI issues royal orders naming settlement near San Miguel mission (near today's Seville Square) 'Presidio San Miguel de Panzacola.'

12-25.text: Christmas abortion bombings: Matt Goldsby and Jimmy Simmons bomb the Ladies Center abortion clinic.

12-28.text: The Pensacola literary journal The Neutral runs its first issue.

12-29.text: Escambia County Sheriff's Deputy James Sullivan allegedly uses excessive force against a subdued suspect, video evidence of which he later destroyed.

12-30.text: Matt Goldsby is arrested after evidence from a trio of abortion-related bombings are traced to his workplace.

12-31.text: Holly Benson resigns as Florida House District 3 representative.
